---
title: "FastEarth3D"
subtitle: "A fast, open-source, time-domain spectral–finite-element solid-Earth model"
toc: false
---

**FastEarth3D** is a state-of-the-art but deliberately simple and fast **3D
solid-Earth model**: a self-gravitating, incompressible, Maxwell-viscoelastic
deformation model coupled to a self-consistent **sea-level equation** with
migrating coastlines. It is designed as an **open-source replacement for
VILMA** within the [CLIMBER-X](https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-15-5905-2022) Earth
system model [@Willeit2022], and more broadly as a reusable glacial isostatic
adjustment (GIA) engine for ice-sheet and sea-level work.

The numerical method is a clean-room reimplementation of the
**spectral–finite-element, time-domain** approach of @Martinec2000: spherical
harmonics horizontally, finite elements radially, and an explicit
memory-stress integration of the Maxwell rheology in time. It is built
**3D-ready from the start** — laterally varying viscosity costs nothing extra
in the solver — yet validated first in 1D against the published GIA community
benchmarks [@Spada2011; @Martinec2018].

::: {.callout-note title="A clean-room implementation"}
FastEarth3D reproduces the *physics* of VILMA — an incompressible,
self-gravitating, Maxwell sphere with rotational feedback and a full
migrating-coastline sea-level equation [@Albrecht2024; @Bagge2021] — entirely
from the **published literature**. No VILMA source code is read or used. Where
this documentation says "Martinec (2000), eq. (80)", that equation was
transcribed from the paper and re-derived independently.
:::

## Why this method

The decisive structural fact behind the method is that, with an **explicit**
Maxwell time scheme, the stiffness solve has **no angular coupling**: each
spherical-harmonic degree $\ell$ decouples into an independent, small,
banded radial system. This makes the solver embarrassingly parallel and cheap,
and — crucially — makes lateral (3D) viscosity *free*: it never couples the
harmonics in the solve, entering only through the explicitly-known memory
stress, which is evaluated pointwise on the Gauss–Legendre grid and transformed
back to spectral space each step (a pseudo-spectral treatment). The **1D and 3D
code paths are therefore the same solver**.

| Method family | Examples | 3D viscosity | Speed | Fit for purpose |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|---|
| Normal-mode + Love numbers + SLE | SELEN, TABOO, ALMA3 | no (1D only) | fast (1D) | dead end for 3D |
| **Time-domain spectral–FE** | **VILMA** [@Martinec2000] | **yes** | **fast** | **chosen** |
| Full 3D FE/FV | CitcomSVE, ASPECT, Elmer | yes | slow, heavy | too complex |

On top of the full time-domain solver, FastEarth3D adds an optional **reduced
[modal solver](physics/modal.qmd)** — the per-degree relaxation collapsed onto a
handful of exponential modes — as a faster, tunable approximation that converges
back to the full solver. It is a dial, not a different method.

## What is in these docs

- **[Install & run](getting-started.qmd)** — building with `configme`, the
  `&fe3d` namelist, the drivers, and staging runs with `runme`.
- **[Physics](physics/governing-equations.qmd)** — the governing equations
  (momentum, Poisson, incompressibility), the Maxwell rheology and its memory
  stress, the [modal solver](physics/modal.qmd) (a tunable reduced
  approximation), the sea-level equation, and rotational feedback.
- **[Discretization](discretization/spectral.qmd)** — the spectral reduction,
  the P1/P0 radial finite-element operator, the explicit time scheme, and the
  banded direct solver.
- **[Benchmarks](benchmarks/index.qmd)** — a summary table of validation
  results, with one page per benchmark (Love numbers, the disc-load response,
  the sea-level-equation intercomparison, and the modal-vs-VE study).
- **[Implementation](implementation.qmd)** — the libraries we build on (SHTns,
  FFTW), the codebases that informed the validation, and the attribution and
  clean-room policy.

## Status

The full model is implemented: the spectral–finite-element solver core, the
viscoelastic time stepping and response operator, the self-consistent
migrating-coastline sea-level equation, rotational feedback (polar motion), and
**laterally varying (3D) viscosity** — together with restart and spin-up, online
remapping of lon-lat forcing, and a host-coupling API. A second response
operator, the reduced **[modal solver](physics/modal.qmd)**, offers a tunable,
faster approximation that converges back to the full solver.

Validation against the Spada et al. (2011) and Martinec et al. (2018) community
benchmarks covers the radial solver, the disc-load response, and the sea-level
equation; rotation is checked against Spada test 3/2. Cross-code validation of
the 3D viscosity path and the [modal-vs-VE accuracy/cost
study](benchmarks/modal-vs-ve.qmd) are ongoing — see the
[validation ladder](benchmarks/index.qmd).
